title: Community AI Training & Translation (IS 482)
power_phrases: We are made of star stuff
frameworks: Feynman Technique: explain simply → find gaps → return to source → simplify again
Cognitive Load Theory (Sweller): working memory holds ~4 items, manage load or lose audience
Freire's Pedagogy: dialogue over banking, co-creation over deposit, lived experience as foundation
Compression Reversal: in Studios 1-7 you compress (precise terms), in Studio 8 you decompress (accessible terms)
CCR for Audiences: draft → deliver to pilot → watch confusion → revise → retest
concepts: Translation is not 'dumbing it down' — it's finding the bridge between what you know and what your audience can imagine
The audience has a biological context window: working memory ~4 items (cognitive load theory)
Shannon's encoding theorem applies both ways: compress for AI, decompress for humans
If you can't explain it simply, you don't understand it well enough (Feynman)
Curse of knowledge: once you know something, you can't remember not knowing it
Change the representation, not the rigor (Resnick/Scratch approach)
The bottleneck to AI adoption is not technology — it's translation
Security literacy for non-technical audiences: phishing, privacy, misinformation, deepfakes
context: IS 482 is Studio 8 (final) of the BSEAI degree. Students deliver live community AI training events for real non-technical audiences through the Center for Enterprise AI. The core capability is Translation — making the invisible visible, bridging expert knowledge to public understanding. Service learning course serving the Newark community.
